one_line_takeaway: ForgeDAN is a black-box evolutionary jailbreak framework combining diversified multi-level text mutation, RoBERTa-embedding semantic fitness, and dual comply/harmful classifier judgment, and its ablation shows the semantic-fitness and judgment modules -- not the mutation diversity -- are what drive its large ASR gains over AutoDAN-HGA, PAIR, GCG, and manual DAN across four LLMs and three evaluation settings.
key_finding: On RQ1 (same-payload attack, Table V), ForgeDAN achieves ASR of 58.65% (DeepSeek-V3), 98.27% (Gemma-2-9B), 87.50% (Qwen2.5-7B), and 55.00% (TranSpec-13B), beating the best baseline on each model by wide margins (e.g., vs. 23.65% for DAN on Gemma-2-9B, 40.58% for PAIR on Qwen2.5-7B). GCG stays below 4% ASR on every model. On RQ2 (cross-payload transfer, Table VI), ForgeDAN ranges 54.23%-98.46% ASR, exceeding the best baseline by over 40 percentage points on Gemma-2-9B and Qwen2.5-7B; AutoDAN-HGA ranges 14.42%-62.88%, DAN 4.42%-40.96%, GCG as low as 0.38%. On RQ3 (real-world proprietary dataset, Table VII), ForgeDAN reaches 100.00% ASR on Gemma-2-9B and 89.05% on Qwen2.5-7B, with 57.66% and 56.20% on DeepSeek-V3 and TranSpec-13B respectively, again ahead of all baselines. The ablation (RQ4, Fig. 3) shows the semantic fitness module is the most critical component: removing it collapses ASR to 5.77% (Gemma-2-9B) and 12.12% (TranSpec-13B); removing the dual-dimensional judgment (replaced by keyword matching) causes ASR to crater on Qwen2.5-7B (87.50% -> 1.92%) and DeepSeek-V3 (58.65% -> 10.19%); removing multi-strategic mutation (reduced to synonym substitution) has only a moderate effect (ASR stays 55.96%-97.88%, and on Qwen2.5-7B the simplified version even slightly outperforms the full system). The paper also reports a complexity comparison: PAIR is O(m*n) worst case and empirically ~10x slower than ForgeDAN's O(m) per-sample parallelized evaluation (tens of minutes vs. minutes per jailbreak).
core_contribution: ForgeDAN is a black-box, gradient-free evolutionary jailbreak framework for aligned LLMs that extends AutoDAN-HGA's genetic-algorithm approach with three specific mechanisms: (1) a multi-strategic, plugin-based mutation library spanning character-level (homoglyph substitution, neighbor swap, char insert/delete/replace), word-level (synonym replacement, morphological change, homophone substitution, paraphrase substitution), and sentence-level (restructuring, clause reordering) operators, replacing AutoDAN-HGA's single-path mutation; (2) a semantic fitness function Fitness(t,goal,hrr) = sim(E(M(t,goal)), E(hrr)) using a RoBERTa text encoder to score cosine similarity between the model's response and a harmful reference response, replacing AutoDAN-HGA's shallow token-level Jaccard/cross-entropy fitness signal; and (3) a dual-dimensional jailbreak judgment mechanism using two fine-tuned classifiers -- C_behavior (refuse vs. comply) and C_content (safe vs. harmful) -- where success is defined only as the logical conjunction comply AND harmful, replacing brittle keyword-matching detection. The full evolutionary loop (Algorithm 1) mutates a seed template t0, filters candidates via a semantic-preservation constraint Sim(t',t0) >= tau, selects the fittest, and iterates until a jailbreak succeeds or Tmax generations are reached (default Tmax=5, population N=10, elite K=2). The prototype was built on top of the open-source garak jailbreak pipeline.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with query-response-only access to the target LLM M: no internal parameters, gradients, or training data. The adversary constructs an adversarial template prefix t (concatenated with a fixed malicious payload 'goal') to maximize P[Bypass(M(t,goal))], subject to a semantic-preservation constraint Sim(t,t0) >= tau so the mutated prompt stays coherent with the original harmful intent t0. Constraints explicitly stated by the authors: prompts must remain natural, stealthy, and human-readable (to evade heuristic/automated detection), consistent with realistic threat conditions against closed-source commercial LLM deployments. Success is formally defined via a jailbreak judgment matrix (Table II): only the 'comply + harmful' outcome counts as a successful jailbreak; refusal or compliant-but-safe outputs do not.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion section (VI) is the source of most caveats it acknowledges: cross-model variability in jailbreak susceptibility cannot be fully explained because pretraining data, alignment strategies, and filtering details are undisclosed for most open-source models, so the authors treat model heterogeneity as an inherent, unresolved uncertainty. ForgeDAN incurs higher computational cost than low-cost baselines (DAN, GCG, AutoDAN-HGA) because it runs full generation+inference+judgment stages every iteration. The ablation shows the mutation-diversity component is not always beneficial (synonym-only mutation occasionally beats full multi-strategic mutation on Qwen2.5-7B), which the authors flag as evidence that excessive/poorly calibrated perturbation can introduce noise and is an area for future refinement. The two judgment classifiers (C_behavior, C_content) are described only as 'fine-tuned classifiers... specialized for orthogonal tasks' with no training-data description, architecture, or standalone accuracy/precision-recall numbers reported for the classifiers themselves -- their reliability is asserted, not independently measured, in this text. The real-world dataset (137 samples) is proprietary and drawn from a single anonymized AI enterprise's logs, limiting external reproducibility. Defenses are discussed only qualitatively (adversarial-augmented alignment, dual-classifier runtime fences, dynamic refusal activation) -- no defense is implemented or evaluated against ForgeDAN in this paper.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 malicious request samples with verified reference responses, from Zou et al. GCG paper; authors further sub-categorized into 7 categories via TF-IDF clustering + manual annotation: profanity 186, dangerous/illegal suggestions 137, cyber-crime 78, misinformation 59, threatening behavior 34, graphic depictions 15, discrimination 11)

Proprietary real-world dataset (137 labeled malicious request samples drawn from operational chat logs of an anonymized AI enterprise, categorized into the same 7 categories: profanity 76, dangerous/illegal 28, cyber-crime 5, misinformation 12, threatening behavior 7, graphic depictions 4, discrimination 5)
quantitative_results: RQ1 direct-attack ASR (Table V): ForgeDAN 58.65% DeepSeek-V3, 98.27% Gemma-2-9B, 87.50% Qwen2.5-7B, 55.00% TranSpec-13B
RQ2 cross-payload transfer ASR (Table VI): ForgeDAN 61.54% DeepSeek-V3, 98.46% Gemma-2-9B, 87.12% Qwen2.5-7B, 54.23% TranSpec-13B
RQ3 real-world ASR (Table VII): ForgeDAN 57.66% DeepSeek-V3, 100.00% Gemma-2-9B, 89.05% Qwen2.5-7B, 56.20% TranSpec-13B

RQ4 ablation ASR on ForgeDAN full vs w/o Mutation vs w/o Fitness vs w/o Judgment (Fig. 3): DeepSeek-V3 58.7% / 56.0% / 16.7% / 10.2%; Gemma-2-9B 98.3% / 97.9% / 5.8% / 19.0%; Qwen2.5-7B 87.5% / 92.9% / 11.3% / 1.9%; TranSpec-13B 55.0% / 61.5% / 12.1% / 19.4%
PAIR worst-case complexity O(m*n) vs ForgeDAN's parallelized O(m) per sample; PAIR empirically ~10x slower (tens of minutes vs minutes per jailbreak)
baselines_compared: GCG (white-box, gradient-based adversarial suffix; Zou et al. ICLR 2023) -- consistently under 4-19% ASR across all three evaluation settings, weakest baseline throughout

AutoDAN-HGA (black-box hierarchical genetic algorithm, DAN-style prefix; Liu et al. ICLR 2024) -- the direct predecessor ForgeDAN extends; moderate but unstable ASR (11.35%-62.88% depending on model/setting)
PAIR (black-box attacker-LLM iterative refinement; Chao et al. SaTML 2025) -- up to 40.58% ASR on RQ1, excluded from RQ2/RQ3 because it cannot generalize to unseen payloads
DAN (manual, human-crafted expert-designed prefixes; Shen et al. 2024) -- up to 51.82% ASR (RQ3, Qwen2.5-7B) but generally lower and less scalable than automated methods
